title: Disaster (Over-)Insurance: The Long-Term Financial and Socioeconomic Consequences of Hurricane Katrina

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This NY Fed staff report (February 2017) examines the long-term socioeconomic impacts of Hurricane Katrina on New Orleans residents using 15 years of credit report data. The study finds that inundated residents had higher insolvency rates and lower homeownership a decade after the hurricane, while residents in the Gulf Opportunity Zone (eligible for federal programs) showed net financial benefits including higher consumption and homeownership rates.
key_points: Authors Zachary Bleemer and Wilbert van der Klaauw use a 5-percent panel of adult Americans' credit reports over 15 years
Ten years post-Katrina, inundated New Orleans residents showed higher rates of insolvency and lower homeownership compared to non-flooded neighbors
White and black neighborhood residents had similar short- and long-term outcomes, though white residents were more likely to migrate out of the city
Inundated residents were more likely to migrate to neighboring states but less likely to migrate north than non-flooded residents
GO Zone residents (eligible for federal programs) obtained net financial benefits with higher consumption, homeownership, and lower bankruptcy/foreclosure rates
Net gains in the GO Zone were progressive, favoring young and low-income residents
why_it_matters: This NY Fed staff report (February 2017) examines the long-term socioeconomic impacts of Hurricane Katrina on New Orleans residents using 15 years of credit report data. The study finds that inundated residents had higher insolvency rates and lower homeownership a decade after the hurricane, while residents in the Gulf Opportunity Zone (eligible for federal programs) showed net financial benefits including higher consumption and homeownership rates.
macro_context: This research provides empirical evidence on the long-term effectiveness of federal disaster insurance programs (FEMA, GO Zone) in mitigating socioeconomic shocks from major hurricanes. The findings inform macroprudential policy discussions about disaster risk transfer and federal response effectiveness. Published February 2017, approximately 9 years before TODAY, but retains archival value as a rigorous empirical study of disaster policy outcomes.
market_structure_context: This source does not address market structure topics. It examines disaster insurance policy and the socioeconomic consequences of Hurricane Katrina on residential outcomes.
important_numbers: 5 percent sample of adult Americans' credit reports
